exemplar.title:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly and preciselyâwith raw and processed tables, units, uncertainties, labels, and error bars.
Data processing steps (averages, inverse volumes, linear regression) are carried out appropriately and accurately, yielding a strong correlation (r = 0.9998).
Sample calculations and consistent use of uncertainty estimates aid transparency and support conclusions.
weaknesses: Propagation of uncertainty through transforms (eg. ÎV â Î(1/âV)) is omitted, understating random errors.
Systematic uncertainties (ambient temperature, app calibration) are ignored despite being conceptually significant.
Graph axes occasionally omit units or labels, and some redundant calculations clutter the presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing meets the 5â6 descriptor (clear and precise). Data processing quality is likewise accurate (5â6), while consideration of uncertainties sits at the top of band 3â4 due to omitted systematic factors. The overall profile (two strands at 5â6, one at 3â4) supports a score of 5.

strengths: Research question is described within a specific and appropriate context (champagne bottle as a Helmholtz resonator).
Clear measurement description of neck length and background details (volume, neck dimensions, water displacement).
Numbered procedure with materials list and photographs supports reproducibility for most steps.
Identification and statement of controlled variables (bottle cleanliness, table surface, quiet room) shows careful planning.
weaknesses: Key procedural details remain ambiguous (precise microphone distance, blowing angle/technique, environmental temperature).
Controlled variables are stated but not quantitatively justified or linked to their impact on data precision.
Some theoretical definitions (eg. v, L in formula) are referenced elsewhere rather than included at point of use.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student excels in situating the research question within a precise physical context (band 5â6) and provides a largely reproducible methodology (band 3â4). Methodological considerations are described but lack the depth of explanation required for full band 5â6. Balancing a topâband context with some ambiguities in procedure and limited explanatory detail justifies a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question with quantitative evidence (frequency range, exponent, correlation).
Quantitative comparison of the measured exponent (â0.4902) with the theoretical âÂ½ shows critical engagement.
Reasoning is consistent with the analysis and uses data effectively to support claims.
weaknesses: Discussion of the nonzero yâintercept and potential systematic offsets is absent.
Statistical indicators (uncertainty ranges, error margins) are not presented alongside conclusions.
Comparison to accepted scientific context is relevant but stops short of fully justifying discrepancies (eg. speedâofâsound variation).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is wellâsupported, consistent, and uses numerical evidence, satisfying the 5â6 band for relevance and support. However, scientific context comparison remains at band 3â4âthere is a comparison but it is not fully justified or detailed. This mix results in a holistic score of 4.

strengths: Realistic improvements (eg. device for resonance, additional personnel) are suggested.
Student recognizes random error sources (volume measurement, blowing consistency).
weaknesses: Methodological weaknesses are stated generically (eg. âno systematic errorâ) without specific impact analysis.
Suggested improvements are listed without explanation of how they address particular errors.
Systematic influences (temperature, microphone response) are not acknowledged in evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands (methodological weaknesses and suggested improvements) reside firmly in the 1â2 band: weaknesses and improvements are stated but lack depth of description or explanation. Consequently, an evaluation score of 2 reflects the superficial treatment of this criterion.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames the research question explicitly within the concrete context of a champagne bottle functioning as a Helmholtz resonator.  Relevant background (neck dimensions, total bottle volume, use of water to displace air) is supplied so the meaning, motivation and physical setting of the investigation are unmistakable.  While the wording of the question is somewhat lengthy, the contextualization is specific and appropriate, satisfying the descriptor for the 5â6 band.

Instrument uncertainties are stated and propagated through to 1/V and fÂ²; error bars and gradient uncertainty are calculated.  Nevertheless, possible systematic uncertainty (e.g. temperature dependence of sound speed, calibration of the app) is ignored and the claim of âno systematic errorâ reveals conceptual omission.  The consideration is solid but not fully appropriate, placing it in the upper part of the 3â4 band.

The student states relevant considerations (clean bottle, constant table surface, microphone location, quiet room) and recognises two uncontrolled factors.  Yet each point is merely described; there is limited explanation of why these controls matter quantitatively or how the uncontrolled factors might influence the data set or be mitigated.  The treatment therefore meets the âdescribedâ level but does not reach the explanatory depth of the 5â6 band.

Methodological weaknesses are asserted superficially: the report claims âno systematic errorâ and only briefly mentions random error from volume measurement and blowing speed without explaining magnitude or impact.  This is essentially a statement of generic issues, corresponding to the 1â2 band.

A numbered procedure, materials list and photographs allow most competent readers to repeat the investigation.  Key volumes, number of trials and the app used for frequency measurement are stated.  However, several practical details remain ambiguous (exact microphone distance, angle/strength of the air jet, environmental temperature), meaning small clarifications would still be required for faithful replication.  This aligns with âfew ambiguities or omissions,â placing the work high in the 3â4 band.

Averages, inverse calculations, linear regression, gradient and intercept uncertainties are executed correctly and consistently with the data.  No significant mathematical or logical errors are evident, so processing quality is appropriate and accurate, matching the 5â6 band.

Improvements such as âuse two peopleâ or âspecial device to induce resonanceâ are realistic but simply listed; the student does not describe how each change would reduce specific errors or enhance data quality.  The descriptor for 1â2 marks (âimprovements are statedâ) therefore applies.

Raw and processed data tables are clearly laid out with units and stated uncertainties; graphs are labelled and include error bars.  Processing steps (averages, squares, inverse volumes) are shown and sample calculations are provided.  Minor formatting issues do not hinder comprehension, so the communication is both clear and precise, fulfilling the 5â6 band.

The student compares the experimental exponent (â0.4902) and the presence of a y-intercept with the theoretical  âÂ½ power law and intercept = 0, discussing agreement and possible error sources.  While relevant, the analysis stops short of quantitatively reconciling gradient with theoretical parameters or discussing speed-of-sound variations, so the comparison is sound but not fully justified, fitting the 3â4 band.

The conclusion directly answers the research question, uses numerical evidence (frequency rise from 126 Hz to 279 Hz, power-fit exponent â0.49, correlation 0.9998) and is entirely consistent with the processed analysis.  The reasoning is explicit and justified, meriting the 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: The measurement description of neck length from shoulder to rim is clear and unambiguous, aiding reproducibility.
Equation for area $$A=\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ should explicitly state units (cmÂ²) within the equation to avoid confusion.
When linearizing, explicitly relate gradient $$m$$ back to physical constants: $$m=\frac{v^2A}{4\pi^2L}$$ to link theory and data.
Expected linear graph lacks axis labels and units. Add clear axis titles (e.g., $f^2$ / HzÂ² vs. $1/V$ / cmâ»Â³) for precise communication.
Figure numbering in the text and captions is inconsistent (âFigure 3â vs âFigure 4â). Correct numbering to avoid confusion.
Ambient temperature directly affects speed of sound $v$. Measure and record temperature to refine theoretical frequency calculations.
Treating uncertainty in $V$ as negligible is not justified; propagate Î$V$ through $1/\sqrt{V}$ and include error bars for completeness.
Calibration of the Phyphox app against a known frequency source would validate measurement accuracy and identify any systematic bias.
Microphone position is described without a measured distance. Specify and record the microphone-to-neck distance to reduce variability.
The research question is situated within a specific and appropriate context, linking air volume manipulation and resonant frequency in a champagne bottle effectively.
The procedure lacks precise details on microphone distance, blowing angle, and environmental temperature. Specify these parameters to ensure full reproducibility.
Controls (clean bottle, table surface, quiet room) are stated but not quantitatively explained. Discuss why each control matters and its influence on data precision.
The description of Helmholtz resonance is concise and clear, effectively introducing the theoretical basis for the resonance phenomenon in a bottle.
At the point of presenting $$f=\frac{v}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{A}{VL}}$$, include definitions of $$v$$ and $$L$$ here so readers need not search elsewhere.
The figure caption lacks publication details (publisher, date). Provide a full citation for the Helmholtz resonator image source.
For the end-correction formula $$L = L_n + 0.3d$$, cite the source or theory underpinning the 0.3 factor to strengthen methodological rigor.
Blowing angle and technique are unspecified in step 5. Standardize or quantify the blowing method for consistent resonance excitation.
Identification of controlled variables (glass state, microphone position, surface) demonstrates careful experimental planning.
Including environmental and ethical concerns shows thorough consideration beyond core methodology.
Raw data table is clearly presented with uncertainties and trial structure, aiding transparency of data recording.
Uncertainty in $1/\sqrt{V}$ is not negligible. Perform proper propagation of Î$V$ to derive Î($1/\sqrt{V}$) and include it.
Caption for Table 2 refers to the wrong variable combination. Update caption to reflect content (average frequency vs. $1/\sqrt{V}$).
Uncertainty in average $f^2$ was calculated via range/2, but a standard deviation approach would better characterize random error.
Sample calculations reference generic labels. Ensure each calculation cites the correct table and variable explicitly.
Frequency vs. volume graph is clearly plotted, showing the trend reliably; error bars enhance trust in data precision.
The nonzero y-intercept warrants discussion of systematic offsets (e.g., calibration, background noise) in the conclusion.
The repeated computation of $1/\sqrt{V}$ for multiple sections leads to redundancy; consolidate and reference earlier results.
Graph 2 shows an excellent linear fit with correlation 0.9998, effectively validating the theoretical model.
Graph axes in the linear plot are too small and omit units. Label both axes clearly (e.g., $1/V$ /cmâ»Â³, $f^2$ / HzÂ²).
The conclusion summary would be strengthened by including statistical indicators (e.g., uncertainty ranges) alongside descriptive statements.
Quantitative comparison of the power-law exponent ($n=-0.4902$ vs $-0.5$) shows strong theoretical agreement and critical analysis.
Express gradient in SI units (HzÂ²Â·mÂ³) rather than cmÂ³ to enhance comparability with standard Helmholtz theory.
Suggest exploring alternative transformations (e.g., logarithmic) or variable combinations to probe sensitivity at very low volumes.
The statement âno systematic errorsâ overlooks effects such as temperature variation and microphone response; discuss these systematic influences.
Listed improvements lack explanation of how each change would mitigate specific errors; expand on mechanism and expected impact.
Future extensions could examine liquid density variation; propose controlled density experiments with measured viscosity and acoustic properties.
The extensions section is truncated mid-sentence; complete the thought on additional bottle variables to provide closure.
Reference formatting is inconsistent (titles, URLs). Use a uniform citation style (e.g., APA) and include missing sources (e.g., Phyphox app).

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
